stem: Two roads of equal status meet with no signs, markings, or traffic lights. Who has priority?

choices.text: The car coming from the left
The car coming from the right
The car going straight on
The car that arrived first

explanation.short: Without signs or markings, traffic coming from the right has priority at an equal-road junction.
explanation.examTip: This rule covers cyclists and mopeds on the road too, not just cars.

stem: You want to turn left at a junction. An oncoming car is going straight on. What must you do?

choices.text: Turn quickly because you arrived first
Give way to the oncoming car
Sound the horn and turn
Turn only if the oncoming car flashes its headlights

explanation.short: When turning left, you must yield to oncoming traffic going straight on.
explanation.examTip: Flashing headlights from another driver is not a legal sign of priority — never base a turn on it.

stem: You are turning right into a side street. A pedestrian is already crossing the side street. You should:

choices.text: Continue — you have priority because you are turning
Sound your horn so the pedestrian hurries
Give way to the pedestrian
Stop only if the pedestrian is in your lane

explanation.short: When turning, you give way to pedestrians already crossing the road you turn into.
explanation.examTip: The same rule applies to cyclists going straight on along the road you are leaving.

stem: You approach a junction with an inverted red-bordered triangle. The crossing road is empty. You must:

choices.text: Stop completely at the line
Yield to crossing traffic, but you may roll through if it is clear
Continue at normal speed because you are alone
Wait for an oncoming car before crossing

explanation.short: A give-way sign requires you to yield, not necessarily to stop fully.
explanation.examTip: If a STOP sign were used instead, you would have to stop completely even when the road is empty.

stem: A traffic light shows green and a priority-road sign points toward your road. A car has not yet cleared the junction. You should:

choices.text: Drive on at speed — green and priority both favour you
Stop and wait until the light turns amber
Give way to the car that is still legally on the junction
Sound your horn and continue

explanation.short: A green light or priority does not let you push into a junction that is not yet clear; yield to road users already on it.
explanation.examTip: Examiners watch for drivers who treat green as an absolute right of way.

stem: A traffic light at a junction is flashing amber. What does this mean for you?

choices.text: Treat the light as red and stop
Treat the light as green and continue
The light is out of service; follow the priority signs and markings
Reverse and find another route

explanation.short: Flashing amber means the signal is not active; you fall back to the priority signs at the junction.
explanation.examTip: If no priority signs are present either, the default 'priority from the right' rule applies.

stem: You are about to enter a typical Dutch roundabout with shark's teeth at the entry. Who has priority?

choices.text: You, because you are about to enter
Traffic already on the ring
Cars on your right approaching the same roundabout
Whoever arrives at the give-way line first

explanation.short: On almost all Dutch roundabouts, traffic on the ring has priority over traffic entering.
explanation.examTip: Shark's teeth at the entry confirm the give-way duty.

stem: Inside a built-up area you leave a roundabout with a ring cycle path. A cyclist on the cycle path is approaching from your left. What is the most likely correct action?

choices.text: Accelerate to clear the exit before the cyclist
Give way to the cyclist on the cycle path
You always have priority because you are in a car
Stop in the middle of the roundabout

explanation.short: In built-up areas, cyclists on the ring cycle path of a roundabout usually have priority over cars entering and leaving.
explanation.examTip: Check the shark's teeth at the cycle crossing — they point at whoever must yield.

stem: When should you indicate when leaving a single-lane roundabout?

choices.text: Always indicate to the left while on the ring
Indicate to the right just before your exit
There is no need to indicate on a roundabout
Indicate only if pedestrians are present

explanation.short: Signal right just before the exit so other road users can predict you are leaving.
explanation.examTip: Failing to indicate at the exit is a common scoring point against learner drivers.

stem: At a marked zebra crossing, a pedestrian is clearly about to step onto the road. You should:

choices.text: Maintain speed because they have not yet stepped on
Slow down and let them cross
Sound the horn so they wait
Stop only if other cars are stopping

explanation.short: Pedestrians crossing or clearly about to cross at a zebra have priority — let them cross.
explanation.examTip: Brake early and smoothly so they feel safe to cross, not pressured by your approach.

stem: An emergency vehicle with blue lights and sirens approaches from behind on a busy two-lane road. You should:

choices.text: Stop dead in your lane immediately
Speed up to stay ahead of it
Move aside safely and, if necessary, stop to let it pass
Ignore it — it must overtake you

explanation.short: Emergency vehicles with sirens and blue lights have priority; make room safely and stop if you must.
explanation.examTip: Do not brake sharply or stop unpredictably — give other drivers room to react too.

stem: Outside of specific signed exceptions, a tram approaches an unsignalled junction at the same time as you from your right. Who has priority?

choices.text: You, because you are a car
The tram, in almost every case
Whoever started across first
Neither — both must stop

explanation.short: Trams generally have priority over other traffic, including over the right-hand priority rule, unless a sign or signal says otherwise.
explanation.examTip: Trams cannot easily brake or swerve; always assume they keep going.

stem: You drive out of a car park onto a public road. Who has priority?

choices.text: You, because you are already moving
All traffic on the public road
Only traffic coming from your right
Only oncoming traffic

explanation.short: Leaving a private area (driveway, car park, petrol station) you must give way to all road users.
explanation.examTip: The same rule applies to leaving a footpath, verge or bicycle path.

stem: A yellow diamond on a white background is shown at the side of the road. What does it mean for you?

choices.text: End of priority road
Start of priority road
Give way ahead
No entry for cars

explanation.short: The yellow diamond marks the start of a priority road; traffic on side roads must give way to you.
explanation.examTip: The same diamond with a black bar through it marks the end of the priority road.

stem: On a multi-lane turbo roundabout you have chosen the correct lane on entry. What should you do once on the ring?

choices.text: Change lanes freely to overtake slower cars
Stay in your chosen lane until your exit
Always use the outer lane only
Move to the inner lane just before exiting

explanation.short: Turbo roundabouts use raised lane separators; you must stay in your lane until the exit you pre-selected.
explanation.examTip: Picking the wrong entry lane usually means going around again rather than crossing a separator.

stem: Inside a built-up area a city bus signals to pull away from a stop. What is required of you?

choices.text: Accelerate to pass before it moves
Let the bus pull out if you can do so safely
Sound your horn so the bus waits
You always have priority over a bus

explanation.short: In built-up areas a bus leaving a stop with its indicator on has priority; brake and let it out if safe.
explanation.examTip: Outside built-up areas this rule does not apply — the bus must wait for a gap.

stem: You are driving down a normal town street. From a petrol station on your right, a car edges out into your lane. There are no signs or markings. What is the correct decision?

choices.text: Let the car go — it is coming from your right, so it has priority
Continue at your speed — you have priority because the car is leaving private land
Stop completely and wave the car out
Accelerate to clear the area quickly

explanation.short: A vehicle leaving private land (driveway, car park, petrol station) has no priority, even if it comes from the right.
explanation.examTip: Stay alert and be ready to brake, but legally you keep priority.

stem: You are on a priority road (yellow diamond, sign B1) and want to turn right at a junction. A cyclist is going straight on next to you on a parallel cycle path with no separate shark's teeth at the cycle crossing. What is the correct action?

choices.text: Turn first — you are on the priority road
Sound your horn and turn
Give way to the cyclist going straight on, then turn
Stop completely in the middle of the junction

explanation.short: Priority on the road does not override your duty to give way to cyclists going straight on across the road you turn into.
explanation.examTip: The 'right hook' is one of the most-tested CBR scenarios — always check the cycle path before turning right.

stem: You are about to enter a built-up area roundabout. Shark's teeth are painted at your entry. A car is approaching on the ring from your left and a cyclist on the ring cycle path is approaching the crossing in front of you. In what order do you yield?

choices.text: Enter first — you arrived first
Yield to the ring car only — cyclists must wait
Yield to the ring car and to the cyclist, then enter
Yield to the cyclist only — cars on the ring must let you in

explanation.short: Inside built-up areas, cyclists on the ring cycle path usually have priority; combined with ring traffic priority, you yield to both.
explanation.examTip: Shark's teeth at your entry confirm you yield — order: ring traffic first, then ring cyclists.

stem: You are stopped at a red light. An ambulance approaches from behind with sirens and blue lights, but there is no legal space to move out of the way without crossing the red light. What should you do?

choices.text: Drive through the red light to let the ambulance pass
Mount the cycle path to clear the lane
Stay where you are — make room only within what is legal
Reverse to give the ambulance space

explanation.short: You must only make space within what is legal — never run a red light or drive onto a cycle path, even for an emergency vehicle.
explanation.examTip: If you cannot legally move aside, stay still — the ambulance driver expects this.

stem: You approach a junction with an octagonal STOP sign. The crossing road is completely empty as far as you can see. What is required of you?

choices.text: Slow to walking pace and roll through because it is empty
Stop fully at the line, even if the road is empty, then proceed when safe
Continue at normal speed if no one is visible
Stop only if another car is approaching

explanation.short: A STOP sign legally requires a full stop at the line every time, even when the crossing road appears empty.
explanation.examTip: Examiners watch for the wheels coming to a complete standstill — a rolling stop counts as a fail.

stem: You have priority at a junction, but traffic on the far side of the crossing is at a standstill and your car would block the junction if you entered. What should you do?

choices.text: Drive in anyway — your priority is absolute
Wait before the junction until you can clear it completely
Stop in the middle of the junction and wait there
Sound your horn to clear the traffic ahead

explanation.short: You must never enter a junction you cannot clear, even with priority — blocking the crossing obstructs other traffic.
explanation.examTip: Wait behind the line until you can see a clear path through to the other side.

stem: On a residential road a stationary bus has stopped and children are getting off. Some are crossing in front of the bus toward your side. What should you do?

choices.text: Pass the bus at normal speed — you have priority
Slow right down, be ready to stop, and give way to children stepping out
Overtake quickly before any child appears
Sound the horn to warn the children

explanation.short: Children behave unpredictably; near a stationary bus you must slow down and be ready to give way.
explanation.examTip: Children stepping out from in front of a bus is one of the classic CBR hazard scenarios.

stem: At a junction the traffic light shows green for you, but a police officer in the middle of the junction is holding up a hand toward your direction. What should you do?

choices.text: Continue — the green light overrules the officer
Stop — instructions from a traffic officer override signals and signs
Sound the horn and drive through carefully
Ignore both and follow the car in front

explanation.short: Directions from a traffic officer take priority over traffic lights, signs and road markings.
explanation.examTip: Order of authority: officer > lights > signs > markings > rules of the road.

stem: You are circulating on a roundabout when an ambulance with sirens and blue lights enters from a side road and needs to join the ring. What is the correct action?

choices.text: Maintain speed — ring traffic always has priority
Slow down or stop safely so the ambulance can join and pass
Brake sharply in front of the ambulance
Speed up to clear the roundabout first

explanation.short: Emergency vehicles with sirens and lights override the normal roundabout priority — yield safely.
explanation.examTip: Adjust gradually so the cars behind you can also react — sudden braking is dangerous.

stem: You approach an unsigned crossroads inside a built-up area. A car arrives from your right and a cyclist from your left at roughly the same time. Who must you let pass first?

choices.text: Only the car from the right — the cyclist must yield to motor traffic
Only the cyclist from the left — cyclists always have priority
Both the car from the right and the cyclist from the right-before-left rule still applies to the car only
The car from the right; the cyclist from the left must yield to you

explanation.short: On an unsigned equal-status junction, right-before-left applies to all drivers, including cyclists. Traffic from the left yields; traffic from the right has priority.
explanation.examTip: Bicycles are 'bestuurders' under Dutch traffic law and follow the same right-before-left rule as cars when no signs apply.

stem: You face shark's teeth at a junction and want to turn right. A car is approaching from your left on the priority road, and a cyclist on a two-way cycle path is approaching from your right on the priority road. What must you do?

choices.text: Yield to the car from the left only; the cyclist from the right must yield to you
Yield to the cyclist from the right only; cross before the car
Yield to both — the cyclist on the priority cycle path and the car
Edge out into the junction so other drivers know your intention

explanation.short: Shark's teeth mean you yield to every road user on the priority road, including cyclists on a parallel cycle path — from either direction.
explanation.examTip: Two-way cycle paths along a priority road often catch drivers out: check both ways for cyclists before turning.

stem: You are already circulating on a single-lane roundabout. A tram is approaching the roundabout from a side road and shows no priority signs against you. What should you do?

choices.text: Stop and let the tram enter — trams always have priority
Continue, because traffic already on the roundabout has priority over traffic entering
Wave the tram driver across to be polite
Stop in the middle of the roundabout and wait for the tram to clear

explanation.short: On a Dutch roundabout, traffic already on the ring has priority over all entering traffic — including trams — unless signs say otherwise.
explanation.examTip: The 'trams always have priority' rule does not apply on roundabouts where the roundabout itself is the priority road.

stem: You approach a zebra crossing. A pedestrian with a white cane and a guide dog is standing at the kerb but has not yet stepped onto the road. What must you do?

choices.text: Continue at normal speed — they have not yet started to cross
Sound the horn so they hear you and wait
Stop and let them cross — visually impaired pedestrians at the kerb count as crossing
Wait only if a second pedestrian is also waiting

explanation.short: A pedestrian with a white cane or guide dog who is clearly about to cross must always be given priority at a zebra crossing, even before stepping onto the road.
explanation.examTip: Never sound the horn at a visually impaired pedestrian — it can disorient them. Stop, and stay stopped until they are fully across.

stem: At a junction your main light is red, but a small green arrow pointing right is lit beside it. A cyclist on the parallel cycle path also has a green light. You want to turn right. What must you do?

choices.text: Turn right immediately because the green arrow gives you priority over the cyclist
Wait for the cyclist to cross first, then turn right while the arrow is still lit
Ignore the green arrow because the main light is red
Reverse to make space and turn after the next phase

explanation.short: A green right-turn arrow permits you to turn, but you must still yield to other road users with a green light — including cyclists on a parallel path.
explanation.examTip: Think of the green arrow as a 'you may, after yielding' permission, never as absolute priority.

stem: You are driving at 50 km/h inside a built-up area. A city bus at a stop ahead switches on its left indicator and starts to edge away from the kerb. You are about 30 metres behind. What must you do?

choices.text: Speed up to pass before the bus pulls fully into the lane
Hold your speed and pass on the right if possible
Slow down and let the bus pull out — inside built-up areas a signalling bus has priority
Stop completely until the bus has finished its route

explanation.short: Inside a built-up area, a bus that signals to leave a stop has priority. You must slow or stop to let it pull away.
explanation.examTip: This rule only applies inside built-up areas (cities and villages). Outside them, the bus must yield to you.

stem: You approach an uncontrolled crossroads (no signs, no markings). Another vehicle is approaching from your right. Who must give way?

choices.text: The vehicle from the right must give way to you because you arrived first
You must give way to the vehicle on your right
The larger vehicle always has priority
Neither — you both stop and wait for a signal

explanation.short: At an uncontrolled intersection, you must give way to vehicles coming from your right — the default right-has-right-of-way rule applies.
explanation.examTip: Remember: left yields to right at uncontrolled intersections, unless a sign or marking says otherwise.

image.alt: Top-down diagram of an uncontrolled crossroads. A blue car (you) approaches from the south heading north. A red car labelled ON YOUR RIGHT approaches from the east heading west.
image.caption: Uncontrolled crossroads — no signs, no markings

stem: You approach an intersection where the traffic light is flashing amber. What does this mean for you?

choices.text: Treat it as a green light and proceed at normal speed
Stop and wait for the light to cycle to green
Proceed with caution, yielding to traffic and pedestrians
The light is broken — bypass via the hard shoulder

explanation.short: A flashing amber light is a warning signal. You may proceed but must do so with caution, giving way to others already in or crossing the intersection.
explanation.examTip: Flashing amber is common at night or low-traffic periods. Treat it like an uncontrolled intersection — proceed only when safe.

stem: You are driving on a priority road (yellow diamond sign) and want to turn right at a junction. A vehicle waiting on the minor road wants to cross. Who has right of way?

choices.text: You — you are on the priority road and retain priority even when turning
The other driver — you lose priority the moment you signal to turn
Whoever reaches the junction first
You must stop and wave the other driver through as a courtesy

explanation.short: A priority road grants right of way at all junctions along it, including while turning. The vehicle on the minor road must continue to wait.
explanation.examTip: Priority road status ends only at a crossed yellow diamond sign. Until then you keep right of way at every junction on that road.

stem: When leaving a roundabout at your intended exit, when is the correct moment to signal right?

choices.text: Before you enter the roundabout
As soon as you enter the roundabout
When you have passed the exit before the one you intend to take
It is not necessary to signal when leaving a roundabout

explanation.short: Signal right once you pass the exit before yours, so other drivers and pedestrians know you are leaving at the next one. Signalling too early confuses traffic at other exits.
explanation.examTip: On a roundabout: signal right only when committed to leaving at the next exit — not before entering or immediately after entering.

stem: The traffic light is green for both you and an oncoming driver. You want to turn left; the oncoming driver is going straight ahead. Who must wait?

choices.text: The oncoming driver — they should anticipate your turn
You — left-turning vehicles must give way to oncoming straight-ahead traffic
Neither — a green light means both can proceed regardless of each other
Whoever signals first has priority

explanation.short: Even with a green light, a left-turning driver must yield to oncoming traffic going straight. A green light is a permission to proceed, not absolute priority.
explanation.examTip: Green means you MAY go if it is safe. It does not override right-of-way obligations to oncoming traffic.

stem: You are about to enter an intersection from a side street. A cyclist approaches from your right on the main road. No traffic lights or signs are present. Who has priority?

choices.text: You — cars always have priority over cyclists
The cyclist — they are on your right and on the main road
The cyclist only if they are moving faster than 10 km/h
You — cyclists must give way to motor vehicles at all intersections

explanation.short: Cyclists are full road users with the same right-of-way rules as cars. This cyclist is on your right — you must give way.
explanation.examTip: Cyclists are not automatically subordinate to cars. Apply the same priority rules to them as to any other vehicle.

stem: You arrive at an unmarked intersection at the same time as a tram coming from your left. Who has right of way?

choices.text: You — the tram is on your left, so you have right of way
The tram — trams always have right of way over motor vehicles at intersections
Whoever arrived at the intersection first
The tram only if it is travelling on tracks set in the middle of the road

explanation.short: Trams always have right of way over motor vehicles at intersections, regardless of which direction they approach from.
explanation.examTip: Trams override the normal right-has-priority rule entirely. If a tram is approaching from any direction, yield.

image.alt: Top-down diagram of an intersection. A blue car (you) approaches from the south. An amber tram with a priority badge approaches from the west heading east — from your left.
image.caption: Tram approaching from your left

stem: You are already on a Dutch roundabout. Another vehicle is waiting to enter from your right. Who has priority?

choices.text: The vehicle waiting to enter — they are on your right
You — vehicles already circulating on the roundabout have priority over entering vehicles
The entering vehicle, because roundabouts give priority to traffic from the right
It depends on the size of the roundabout

explanation.short: On Dutch roundabouts, vehicles already circulating have priority over those entering — even if the entering vehicle is on your right. Shark-tooth markings at entries confirm who yields.
explanation.examTip: Dutch roundabout rule: circulating traffic has priority. Look for haaientanden (shark teeth) at the entry — they mark the give-way line for entering drivers.

image.alt: Schematic Dutch roundabout showing a red car already circulating clockwise on the ring and a blue car (you) waiting to enter from the bottom with give-way markings.
image.caption: Dutch roundabout — shark-teeth give-way markings at the entry

stem: A pedestrian steps onto a zebra crossing as you approach at 40 km/h. You are 25 metres away. What must you do?

choices.text: Sound the horn to warn them, then continue
Slow to 30 km/h and pass if they are still on the far half of the crossing
Brake and give way — a pedestrian on a zebra crossing has priority
Flash your headlights and maintain your speed

explanation.short: A pedestrian who has stepped onto a zebra crossing has priority. You must brake and give way, regardless of which half of the crossing they are on.
explanation.examTip: Dutch law requires you to give way to any pedestrian already on the zebra crossing — there is no exception for the far half.

stem: You have right of way at a junction. Another driver clearly fails to give way and enters your path. What should you do?

choices.text: Maintain your speed — you have right of way and the other driver is at fault
Brake and be prepared to stop to avoid a collision, even though you have right of way
Sound the horn loudly and accelerate to assert your priority
Swerve into the oncoming lane to go around them

explanation.short: Having right of way does not give you permission to cause a collision. You must always be prepared to brake and yield to prevent an accident, even when the other driver is at fault.
explanation.examTip: Right of way does not override the duty to prevent harm — this principle appears regularly on the CBR exam.
